conversation.content:
The web search returned nothing useful. I'll rely on the established biophysical principle I already know: in the Knowles/Cohen amyloid master-equation framework, aggregate mass grows via primary nucleation, elongation, and secondary (surface-catalyzed) nucleation, with saturation as monomer depletes. Inhibitors act by suppressing these microscopic rate constants (competitive monomer sequestration and rate suppression), which delays sigmoid onset (right-shifts crossings). This is exactly the breakthrough idea. Let me implement it.

The key structural change: fully autonomous RHS, capacity-driven saturation, competitive monomer sequestration in `free_m`, and inhibitor-suppressed autocatalytic secondary nucleation on the *rate*.
